summary: OWASP NodeGoat is a deliberately vulnerable Node.js/Express web application using MongoDB, designed to demonstrate OWASP Top 10 risks. It handles authentication, session management, and multiple CRUD operations with minimal input validation. Dependencies include known-vulnerable versions of libraries (marked 0.3.5, bcrypt-nodejs 0.0.3, needle 2.2.4).

surfaces.description: Authentication endpoint accepting username/password. Potential for injection attacks against MongoDB query and timing-based enumeration.
User registration endpoint. Accepts untrusted input for user creation in MongoDB with bcrypt-nodejs (unmaintained, known vulnerable).
User profile display page. May reflect user-controlled data without proper output encoding, risking stored XSS.
Profile update endpoint accepting user-controlled fields. Risk of mass assignment, NoSQL injection, and privilege escalation if role/admin fields are writable.
Contributions listing page. May expose other users' data through insecure direct object reference (IDOR).
Contributions submission endpoint. User-supplied numeric/string data written to MongoDB without validation.
Allocations retrieval by user ID parameter. Classic IDOR vulnerability — userId parameter may not be validated against session user.
Allocation creation endpoint accepting financial data. Risk of NoSQL injection and business logic bypass.
Memos listing page rendering user content through marked 0.3.5 (known XSS vulnerabilities in markdown rendering).
Memo creation endpoint. User-supplied markdown content stored and later rendered via vulnerable marked library, enabling stored XSS.
Benefits page potentially exposing sensitive employee data. May have broken access control allowing unauthorized viewing.
Benefits update endpoint. Risk of authorization bypass and injection if benefit amounts or parameters are not validated.
Research page that makes external HTTP requests via needle library. Potential SSRF if URL parameter is user-controlled.
External HTTP requests made via needle 2.2.4. If request URL or parameters are derived from user input, this enables SSRF attacks against internal services.
Users collection storing credentials and profile data. Query construction from user input without parameterization enables NoSQL injection.
Financial allocations collection. Direct use of user-supplied IDs in queries without ObjectId validation enables injection.
Contributions collection with potential for NoSQL injection through unvalidated query parameters.
Application configuration including MongoDB connection strings, session secrets, and environment-specific settings. Hardcoded secrets and insecure defaults.
Session management configuration. express-session setup may use insecure defaults (no secure flag, weak secret, missing httpOnly/sameSite attributes).
Database reset/seed script with direct MongoDB operations. If exposed or callable, could destroy production data.
Server-side template rendering via Swig (deprecated, unmaintained). Templates may render user input without auto-escaping, enabling server-side template injection or XSS.
